description_long: This object is a cluster of five irregularly shaped rocks, with the largest rock positioned at the back and several smaller rocks grouped in front. The rocks have a faceted, polygonal appearance with visible angular edges and flat surfaces, giving them a stylized, low-poly look. Their surfaces are predominantly a warm, sandy brown color, with subtle shading and darker crevices that suggest natural weathering and minor surface cracks. Some rocks display faint, etched markings or scratches, adding to their visual interest and hinting at possible human or environmental interaction. The overall form is compact and grounded, with the rocks closely nestled together, making the group appear as a single, cohesive barrier. This arrangement is typical of decorative or functional rock clusters found in urban parks, landscaped medians, or as naturalistic obstacles along sidewalks and pathways.
description: A cluster of five sandy brown, faceted rocks with angular surfaces and subtle etched markings, arranged as a compact urban barrier.
description_view_0: Three main rocks are visible, with the largest at the back and two smaller ones in front, showing faint surface markings.
description_view_1: All five rocks are visible, including the smallest rock in the foreground, highlighting the full clustered arrangement.
description_view_2: The tallest rock dominates the view, with the smallest rock set apart to the left, emphasizing the height difference.
description_view_3: The rear view shows three main rocks with visible cracks and crevices, presenting a more unified, solid barrier.
attribute_location: urban park or landscaped sidewalk median
attribute_integration_with_urban_infrast: There is no visible evidence of intentional integration with urban infrastructure.
